questions.question: The main benefit of transfer learning is:
options: Reusing learned representations from a large dataset for a related task with less data
No need for labelled data at all
questions.explanation: Pre-trained features (edges, textures, semantic concepts) reduce data and compute requirements for new tasks.

questions.question: Feature extraction in transfer learning means:
options: Freezing all pre-trained layers and only training a new classification head
questions.explanation: With frozen weights, only the new head (typically 1-2 FC layers) is trained. Fast and effective for small datasets.

questions.question: Fine-tuning differs from feature extraction by:
options: Unfreezing some or all pre-trained layers and training with a low learning rate
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning allows gradual adaptation of pre-trained features to the new domain — more powerful but requires more data.

questions.question: Why use a very low learning rate when fine-tuning?
options: To avoid destroying the pre-trained knowledge (catastrophic forgetting)
questions.explanation: High learning rate overwrites pre-trained weights too aggressively. Very low lr (1e-5) makes small, careful adjustments.

questions.question: Domain adaptation handles the case when:
options: Training and test data are from the same distribution
Source domain (ImageNet) and target domain (medical images) have different distributions
Labels are unavailable in both domains
questions.explanation: Domain shift = different data distributions. Domain adaptation techniques help transfer knowledge across this gap.

questions.question: ImageNet pre-training transfers well to medical imaging because:
options: Medical images are identical to ImageNet
Low-level features (edges, textures, gradients) are universal across visual domains
The number of classes is similar
questions.explanation: Despite domain difference, edge/texture detectors learned from natural images are useful for medical images too.

questions.question: Progressive unfreezing in fine-tuning means:
options: Unfreezing layers gradually from top to bottom during training
questions.explanation: Start by training only the head, then unfreeze the last block, then earlier blocks. Each stage uses a lower lr.

questions.question: Knowledge distillation is a form of transfer learning where:
options: A small student model learns from the soft predictions of a large teacher model
Only the first layer is transferred
questions.explanation: Teacher's soft probabilities contain rich inter-class relationships — the student learns these 'dark knowledge' patterns.

questions.question: Few-shot learning aims to:
options: Use the largest possible training set
Generalise to new classes with only a few labelled examples per class
questions.explanation: Few-shot models leverage meta-learning or metric learning to quickly adapt to new tasks from very few examples.

questions.question: Which freezing strategy is typically best for a small target dataset?
options: Freeze all layers, train only the head (feature extraction)
questions.explanation: Small dataset + full fine-tuning = overfitting. Frozen backbone acts as a fixed feature extractor — safer choice.
